text: The patient is positioned in lithotomy after induction of general anesthesia. A three-centimeter transumbilical incision is made, and a surgical glove port is used as the single-access device. Disposable trocars are tied into the glove fingers, and the glove cuff is stretched onto a wound protector retractor placed in the incision. Carbon dioxide insufflation is initiated through one of the trocars to establish pneumoperitoneum.

The laparoscopic view through the glove port reveals an anastomotic stricture with creeping fat visible on the distal ileum. The surgical field shows a relative absence of postoperative peritoneal adhesions.

The surgeon retracts the omentum capillate above the transverse colon with the patient positioned head down and right side up. The affected segment is mobilized from lateral to medial, preparing for an extracorporeal anastomosis facilitated by the three-centimeter single port access site.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileocolonic segment to the midline, preparing to remove the glove port from the wound retractor protector and exteriorize the bowel. The ileocolonic anastomosis is visibly adhered to the lateral peritoneum. The surgeon uses steady readjustment of retracting instruments and effective tissue dissection with a ligature to make progress, avoiding direct grasping of the bowel and instead using closed instrument retraction or gentle traction on the mesentery or mesocolon.

The surgeon utilizes a single port laparoscopic approach, focusing on one instrument and maneuver at a time. A 30-degree lens is used to minimize clashes between the camera and operating instruments, with high-definition optics provided by the EndoEye system from Olympus, featuring in-line sterile optical cabling to reduce exterior instrument clutter.

The surgeon utilizes the surgical glove port, composed of thin, flexible, elastic material, to aid instrument movements. During lateral dissection, the gonadal vessels and right ureter are visualized, ensuring preservation of the fascia overlying these structures.
In the slim patient, the vermiculation of the bowel is clearly visible, indicating active peristalsis within the intestinal tract.

The surgeon retracts the omentum cephalad and elevates the peritoneal attachments towards the anterior abdominal wall, allowing the transverse colon to descend due to gravity. The proximal transverse colon is mobilized to facilitate distal mobilization for extracorporeal staple anastomosis. The duodenum is visible and protected during the procedure, with the camera providing an off-centered view typical of single-port laparoscopic operations.

The surgeon mobilizes the omentum off the transverse colon, extending the dissection beyond the falciform ligament. Attention is then returned to the base of the distal ileal mesentery to ensure sufficient mobilization of IST-II. The position of the ureter is carefully checked, as it may be distorted from previous surgery, posing a risk of injury.
The surgeon divides the final piece of post-operative fibrous tissue and proceeds to dissect a virgin tissue plane, ensuring excellent mobilization of the disease segment.

The surgeon returns the small and large bowel to their original anatomical positions, ensuring correct orientation for direct extraction without twisting. The drug port is removed from the wound protector, and laparoscopic graspers are replaced with Babcock tissue holding forceps. The outer ring of the wound protector is retracted to create a snug fit, allowing for maximum extraction aperture. The bowel is then easily withdrawn, and the strictured anastomosis is clearly visible.

Following the resection of the diseased segment, the surgeon constructs a stapled side-to-side ileocolonic anastomosis. Relaparoscopy is performed to confirm hemostasis and assess the correct alignment of the anastomosis, with the surgical field appearing essentially bloodless.
